coredata.dc:description: “Waste” is “Wealth”. Recovering valuable materials from excess sludge (ES) is trending as an efficient strategy that synergizes waste management and resource recovery. Targeting the inherent composition characteristics of ES, this study aimed to explore resource recovery strategies that can exert its unique advantages. ES from paper mill was unprecedently selected as the carbon precursor, and ES-derived carbon quantum dots (ESCQDs) were successfully synthesized via a facile and one-pot hydrothermal reaction without pretreatments, catalysts or any additives. Notably, its fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and mass recovery rate were as high as 28.472 % and 2.86 %, respectively, which were at a high level among biomass-derived CQDs. N-Ca was successfully co-doped into ESCQDs without any nitrogen or calcium amendment, which has been reported rarely. The effect of Ca doping on the enhancement of QY of CQDs was revealed, thereby revealing the natural advantageous properties of ES from paper mill in the synthesis of high fluorescent Ca-N co-doped CQDs. Furthermore, the effect of the preparing ESCQDs on the environmental risks control of ES were also investigated. This resource recovery process efficiently removed residual heavy metals from ES and significantly reduced and controlled the environmental risk of heavy metals in ES, and the synthetic ESCQDs had low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. This study proposed a green alternative that combines the recovery of value-added products from ES with heavy metal control, provided more in-depth understanding for the formation mechanism of CQDs, and revealed structural strategies for the preparation of high fluorescent CQDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: “Waste” is “Wealth”. Recovering valuable materials from excess sludge (ES) is trending as an efficient strategy that synergizes waste management and resource recovery. Targeting the inherent composition characteristics of ES, this study aimed to explore resource recovery strategies that can exert its unique advantages. ES from paper mill was unprecedently selected as the carbon precursor, and ES-derived carbon quantum dots (ESCQDs) were successfully synthesized via a facile and one-pot hydrothermal reaction without pretreatments, catalysts or any additives. Notably, its fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and mass recovery rate were as high as 28.472 % and 2.86 %, respectively, which were at a high level among biomass-derived CQDs. N-Ca was successfully co-doped into ESCQDs without any nitrogen or calcium amendment, which has been reported rarely. The effect of Ca doping on the enhancement of QY of CQDs was revealed, thereby revealing the natural advantageous properties of ES from paper mill in the synthesis of high fluorescent Ca-N co-doped CQDs. Furthermore, the effect of the preparing ESCQDs on the environmental risks control of ES were also investigated. This resource recovery process efficiently removed residual heavy metals from ES and significantly reduced and controlled the environmental risk of heavy metals in ES, and the synthetic ESCQDs had low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. This study proposed a green alternative that combines the recovery of value-added products from ES with heavy metal control, provided more in-depth understanding for the formation mechanism of CQDs, and revealed structural strategies for the preparation of high fluorescent CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Excess sludge is the by-product of municipal wastewater treatment. With the rapid global expansion of urbanization, the quantity of excess sludge has been increasing steadily over past decades. In China, almost 60 million tons of wet sludge are being produced at wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) per year [1]. Such staggering growth of excess sludge production urges the need for appropriate disposal and management. From a science perspective ( Fig. 1(a)), the issue of excess sludge stimulates research advancements in directions that encompass anaerobic digestion, sludge reduction, sludge cost, and resource recovery.

Sewage and/or sludge can be regarded as a resource with management and treatment. As reported, wastewater contains more than 50 % of lost/waste resources. [2]. From 2011–2019, research on resource recovery from wastewater has accounted for escalating proportions in sludge treatment and management (Fig. 1 (b)). Upcoming excess sludge management efforts are accentuated in resource recovery. Existing efforts focus on the recovery of nutrients, heavy metals, bio-plastics, proteins and enzymes from excess sludge [3]. However, some obstacles like high cost and low market acceptance remain to hamper the scaling up of the production of the recovered materials. Continuously exploring and identifying a new recovered product with high market demand and high economic value will still be a focus in the future investigations.

As the wastewater treatment proceeded, large amounts of heavy metals, pathogens and other pollutants will accumulate in the excess sludge. If the excess sludge is discharged without proper treatment, the accumulation of heavy metals in the food chain will cause serious pollution to the environment and threaten human health [4]. The ecological risk assessment and control of heavy metals in excess sludge is an important problem that cannot be ignored. In the future research on the resource recovery of excess sludge, apart from considering the recovery value and recovery yield, it is also significant to assess the effect of controlling the ecological risk of heavy metals during the recovery process.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel class of zero-dimensional fluorescence carbon-based nanoparticles, have quantum confinement effects and edge effects. Since the first discovery in 2004 [5], driven by their superior optical properties (e.g., multicolored fluorescence, robust photostability, tunable photoluminescence), high biocompatibility, ease of modification, facile synthesis, abundant low-cost sources [6], CQDs have gradually shown their advantages in the fields of optoelectronic devices, biomedicine, sensing, photocatalysis, anticounterfeiting, etc [7], raising considerable scientific interests. Accordingly, market demand for CQDs also showed significant increase [8].

With the development of CQDs, great efforts have been focused on the synthesis of CQDs. Green synthesis of CQDs is our research goal, including green methods and green precursors. A variety of CQD synthesis methods have emerged in the past few years, including laser ablation, chemical oxidation, pyrolysis, microwave, ultrasonic, hydrothermal, etc [9,10]. Laser ablation is a top-down method where the surface state of the prepared CQDs can be tuned and can be used to synthesize different types of nanostructures. Pyrolysis is a high temperature carbonization method to prepare CQDs, and so far, it has become one of the common methods to prepare CQDs. However. laser ablation and pyrolysis are disadvantageous due to complex synthetic condition and high-energy consumption. Chemical oxidation is an effective method for large-scale preparation of CQDs, usually without complex equipment. However, it requires strong acid, which does not align with the goal of green synthesis [11]. Microwave method is a fast, convenient and environmentally friendly preparation method. However, it is poor at controlling the size of CQDs and is not suitable for large-scale synthesis [10]. Ultrasonic method, a method of decomposing large-size carbon materials into CQDs with the help of high energy of ultrasonic waves, can be used to prepare CQDs on a large scale. However, the ultrasonic method is generally suitable for top-down preparation, i.e., the fragmentation of large-size carbon materials (e.g., graphene) into small sizes, and is not suitable for bottom-up preparation, thus limiting its application in waste synthesis scenarios. Moreover, the CQDs obtained by this method are poorly monodispersed [10]. Compared with these methods, the hydrothermal method has the advantages of wide range of application scenarios, eco-friendly, mild reaction conditions, and is suited for scaling up [9]. Therefore, the hydrothermal method is a superior choice for the green synthesis of CQDs. However, to improve fluorescence quantum yield, catalysts (strong oxidants, metal, etc.) or additives are needed in the hydrothermal treatments reported in recent studies [12,13], which does not fully comply with the green synthetic principles.

In the selection of green precursors, waste biomass is advantageous due to its low cost, renewable, and non-toxic properties. Previous studies have synthesized CQD using various types of waste biomass, such as orange waste peel [14], algae [11], prawn shells [15], rice straw [16], and corncob residues [17]. Waste biomass usually contains multifarious heteroatoms. Heteroatom doping can be naturally formed during the synthesis process and various surface groups (amine, hydroxyl, carboxyl, etc) are therefore present on the surface of the synthesized CQDs, enhancing their fluorescence quantum yield [18]. The common doping forms of biomass-derived CQDs mainly include nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus doping, while calcium doped CQDs have been rarely reported [19,20]. Recently, calcium-doped carbon dots gradually exhibit their unique advantageous properties. As previous studies revealed, in addition to the excellent photoluminescence properties of common carbon dots (CDs) (e.g. good water solubility, superior optical properties), calcium-doped CQDs exhibit their own unique and unparalleled properties, especially remarkable fluorescence thermosensitivity, pH-sensitive fluorescence, and excellent chemical stability [21,22]. These decisive characteristics of calcium-doped CQDs create irreplaceable advantages for their applications in pH- or temperature-sensitive optics and bioimaging. Therefore, If calcium doping can be formed, it will be beneficial to the improvement of the photostability, chemical stability and thermal stability of CQDs, which will facilitate its subsequent application. In the meanwhile, for all biomass-derived CQDs, the obstacles including low fluorescence quantum yield and low mass yield still remain in them [23], especially compared to chemical precursors-derived CQDs.

As a kind of waste biomass, excess sludge consists of a myriad of organic matters containing numerous elements (e.g., C, N, S, O, metals, etc.), which can be considered as an abundant “element library” that provides all sorts of element sources for heteroatom doping. Among different types of excess sludge, excess sludge from paper mill is characterized by high calcium content, which has the potential to form calcium doping. Thus, we hypothesize that excess sludge can be a potential advantageous precursor for calcium-doped CQD green synthesis.

For the first time, excess sludge from paper mill was unprecedently employed as a carbon precursor to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via the one-pot hydrothermal treatment without catalysts and additives in this study. At the same time, the optical, morphological, structural properties of synthesized ES-derived CQDs (ESCQDs) were characterized, and its possible formation process was analyzed by tracking changes in their chemical structures and fluorescence properties. To investigate the synthesis efficiency and ecological risk of ESCQDs, the fluorescence quantum yield and mass recovery rate were estimated, and the cytotoxicity of ESCQDs was used to evaluate their biocompatibility and biotoxicity. This study focused on the inherent composition characteristics and potential risks of excess sludge, identified an effective and green strategy that can not only exert its unique advantages and also control its environmental risks at the same time. Moreover, three pathways that could enhance the fluorescence of CQDs were found, providing theoretical support for the preparation of CQDs with high fluorescence performance.

The excess sludge was collected from a paper mill wastewater treatment plant in Zhejiang Province, China. Excess sludge from urban sewage treatment plant, activated sludge in secondary sedimentation tank, secondary sedimentation tank sewage and wastewater influent were collected from an urban wastewater treatment plant in Zhejiang Province, China. Electroplating sludge was collected from an electroplating sewage treatment plant in Zhejiang Province, China. Before the experiment, the above sludge was dried in an oven at 105 ℃ , pulverized and ground to powder, and then sieved with a 40 mesh screen to obtain reaction precursors for use.
Lemons were peeled and squeezed into juice for use.

The corn cobs were collected from a factory in Zhejiang Province, China. The corn cobs were dried in an oven at 105 ℃ , pulverized and grinded to powder, and then sieved with a 40 mesh screen to obtain reaction precursors for use.

As shown in Figs. 2, 1.0 g of excess sludge was dispersed in deionized water at a select solid-liquid ratio (1:10, 2:10, 3:10, 4:10, 5:10) and stirred to achieve an uniform slurry. The mixed slurry was transferred into a 50-mL stainless steel autoclave for the hydrothermal reaction at 200 ℃ (considering the heat resistance limit of the conventional reactor and the safety of the experiment, we chose the highest temperature that the autoclave can withstand 200 ℃ as the reaction temperature), and reacted for 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, 42, 48 h, respectively. After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. The obtained brown solution was ultrasonicated for 1 h and then centrifuged at 20,000g for 1 h. The obtained supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μ m organic filter membrane, then freeze-dried to powder to obtain the ESCQDs for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of lemon juice-, corn cob-and sewage/sludge-derived CDs

ce:section.ce:para: Processed corn cob powders (1.0 g) were dispersed in deionized water at a solid-liquid ratio of 1:10, and stirred to form uniformly mixed slurry. The slurry was added into a 50-mL stainless steel autoclave for the hydrothermal reaction at 200 ℃ for 12 h. After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. The obtained brown solution was firstly dispersed ultrasonically for 1 h by bath- type ultrasonic cleaner (X0–5200DTS, 300 W, 45 kHz), then centrifuged at 20,000g for 1 h. The obtained supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μ m organic filter membrane to remove incomplete reaction macromolecule particles and impurity ions, and then freeze-dried to powder to obtain the corn-cob-derived CDs for use.

30 mL lemon juice was added into a 50-mL stainless steel autoclave for the hydrothermal reaction at 200 ℃ for 12 h. The follow-up operations were the same as the above corn-cob-derived CDs preparation process.

30 mL sewage (secondary sedimentation tank sewage/ wastewater influent) was added into a 50-mL stainless steel autoclave for the hydrothermal reaction at 200 ℃ for 12 h. The follow-up operations were the same as the above corn-cob-derived CDs preparation process.
The operations were the same as the above excess sludge-derived CQDs preparation process.

The fluorescence spectra of the synthesized CDs were measured using an F-4600 FL Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Ltd, Japan). The UV–vis spetra were measured by UV-2600 UV–vis Spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Ltd, Japan). The morphology of the synthesized CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-1400 Flash) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100 Plus). The lattice structure of the ESCQDs was examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, X′PERT PRO X-ray diffractometer) using the CuK α radiation. The surface functional groups of ESCQDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS50, Ltd, China). The surface element morphology of the ESCQDs was obtained by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) using the AlK α radiation source.

HEK-293 T cells were maintained in DMEM medium prepared with fetal bovine serum: Glutamine: Non-essential amino acids at a ratio of 88:10:1:1. The HEK-293 T cells were harvested in a logarithmic growth phase at 37 ℃ in 5 % CO2 humidified environment and then diluted to the cell concentration to 4

The cytotoxicity of the ESCQDs was investigated using the MTT assay with HEK-293 T cells. HEK-293 T cells (100 μ L per well) were exposed to different concentrations of ESCQDs (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 mg/mL) in a 96-well plate and incubated for 24 h at 37 ℃ in 5 % CO2 humidified environment. After the removal of the culture medium, cells in each well were washed twice with PBS, amended with a medium containing 10 % MTT at 200 μ L/well, and incubated for 4 h at 37 ℃ in 5 % CO2 humidified environment. Microplate reader (TECAN, SPARK 10 M) was used to detect the absorbance at 570 nm. Each experiment was repeated three times. The cell viability rate was calculated by the following equation (3):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesized carbon quantum dots from excess sludge
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained solution synthesized from excess sludge was brown under daylight light and emitted bright blue fluorescence under UV illumination ( Fig. 3(a)). This photoluminescence (PL) effect is an important feature that distinguishes carbon dots from other carbon nanomaterials. The observation of bright blue fluorescence indicated the formation of carbon dots (CDs) from excess sludge. Compared with the blue fluorescence that emitted from lemon juice-derived CDs (L-CDs) (Fig.S1(a)) and corn cobs-derived CDs (C-CDs) (Fig.S1(b)), ESCQDs exhibited much stronger fluorescence intensity. The corresponding fluorescence intensity of ESCQDs was far higher than that of C-CDs as well as L-CDs (Fig.S1(c)), supporting its superior fluorescence properties. Moreover, ESCQDs also performed better in fluorescence stability than the C-CDs. As shown in Fig.S1(d), after 15 days, the fluorescence intensity of C-CDs was greatly reduced, while the fluorescence loss of ESCQDs were less (L-CDs fluorescence was completely quenched after 15 days and thus the data were not depicted in the figure. Even after 5 months (stored at room temperature and not protected from light), the fluorescence intensity of ESCQDs remained at high level (Fig. S1(e)), while that of C-CDs was completely quenched at that time. Furthermore, ESCQDs have excellent water solubility, since the solutions dispersed was transparent and uniform, as shown in Fig. 3(a). Therefore, ESCQDs showed excellent fluorescence properties in regards of intensity, stability, and water solubility.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were utilized to further characterize the morphology and microstructure of ESCQDs. The synthesized ESCQDs were uniformly distributed with the particle size of 30–40 nm. They were mostly spherical and well dispersed without apparent aggregation (Fig. 3(b)). Compared with L-CDs and C-CDs, ESCQDs’ particle sizes were 3–4 times smaller. Their boundary was cleaner, and exhibited the best monodispersity unlike L-CDs that showed apparent agglomerations (Fig. S2(a)–(d)). Therefore, ESCQDs possess superior morphological properties with small particle size and high monodispersity. The XRD pattern of ESCQDs exhibited a peak at 22° (Fig. 3(c)), corresponding to the graphite structure with high crystallinity [24]. Furthermore, highly crystallized frameworks with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm were intuitively and clearly observed in Fig. 3(d), which further demonstrated the graphite structure in the ESCQDs [25]. The sp2 hybridized graphitic core is the major characteristic of CQDs that distinguishes them from other types of carbon dots. Herein, the presence of graphite structure validated the formation of CQDs from excess sludge.

To reveal the optical properties of excess sludge-derived CQDs (ESCQD), three-dimensional fluorescence map and UV–vis absorption were investigated in detail. As illustrated in Fig. 4(a), two fluorescence centers could be obviously found, it indicated that the ESCQDs was characterized by double excitation center. The two excitation peaks are located at 235 nm and 325 nm, respectively, but the maximum emission wavelength of them were all located at 405 nm, which agreed with its bright blue color under UV light (Fig. 3(a)). Usually biomass CDs have only one excitation center, so the dual excitation centers of ESCQDs here was very special and interesting, this may be due to the presence of two luminescence centers in ESCQDs, or a single luminescence center that corresponds to the maximum for both excitation wavelengths. As depicted in Fig. 4(b), two distinct broad bands at 265 and 323 nm, corresponding to
π − π
* transitions of CC bonds and n
− π
* transitions of CO surface groups [5], respectively, indicating the presence of CC and CO bonds.

As known, hydrothermal temperature, hydrothermal time, and solid-liquid ratio are the three main factors affecting the optical properties of ESCQDs when the hydrothermal method is employed. High hydrothermal temperature can enhance the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. Therefore, we chose the high hydrothermal temperature of 200 °C for the synthesis of ESCQDs. To further investigate the influence of other hydrothermal conditions on the optical properties of ESCQDs, different hydrothermal time and solid-liquid ratios were used for comparison. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the hydrothermal time was constant (12 h), when the solid-liquid ratio was 2:10, the fluorescence quantum yield of ESCQDs was the highest, which was 16.645 %. It indicated that the optimal solid-to-liquid ratio required for the synthesis of ESCQDs with high fluorescence quantum yield was 2:10. As shown in Fig. 4(c), when the solid-to-liquid was constant (2:10), with the increase of the hydrothermal time, the fluorescence quantum yield of ESCQDs continued to increase and reached the maximum at 36 h, which was 28.472 %. It indicated that the longer hydrothermal time can improve the fluorescence intensity of ESCQDs. The UV absorption peak at 265 nm became stronger as the hydrothermal time increased (Fig.S3(a)), corresponding to the increase of CC bonds. The formation of CC bonds could be attributed to the increased degree of carbonization with the increase of hydrothermal time. The UV absorption peak at 265 and 323 nm became stronger as the solid-liquid ratio increased (Fig.S3(b)), corresponding to the increases of CC and CO bonds. This was probably resulted from the radiative recombination of the excited electrons from the n
− π
* transitions of surface emission trap states [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and mass recovery rate
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quantum yield is a vital indicator evaluating the photoluminescence characteristics of CQDs. In our study, as shown in Table 1, eight precursors were selected for the same reaction, after a one-pot hydrothermal reaction at 200 ℃ without additional reactants or catalysts. Obviously, the QY of ESCQD (QY=16.65 %) is much higher than that of CDs synthesized from other raw materials. Therefore, under the identical synthesis conditions, the photoluminescence performance of ESCQDs was the highest among the eight CDs, which once again proved its advantage as a precursor.

Moreover, the mass recovery rate of ESCQDs were also evaluated. Since 286 mg ESCQDs can be synthesized and recovered from every 10.0 g excess sludge, the mass recovery rate was estimated as 2.86 %. Compared to the large-scale synthesis (with mass recovery rate ∼0.12 %) of food waste-derived CQDs as reported in previous studies [29], thus this study achieved new progress in the large-scale synthesis of waste biomass-derived CQDs. Therefore, excess sludge was an advantageous precursor for the synthesis of high fluorescence quantum yield CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ecological risk assay of ESCQDs synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The inherent environmental pollution risk of heavy metal from excess sludge (ES) itself makes it particularly necessary to assess the ecological risk of heavy metals in the process of preparing CQDs. RAC is a commonly used method for potential environmental risks assessment of heavy metals. Specifically, the proportion of weak acid soluble state in the total heavy metal is used as the risk assessment index [4]. In the meanwhile, to further evaluate the ecotoxicity of heavy metal in ES, the contamination factor (CF) index was also investigated. The specific assessment rules of RAC and CF are shown in Table S1. Based on the CF index values and RAC values in Table 2, the toxicity levels of 5 heavy metals were Cr (moderate contamination), Ni (moderate contamination), Cu (moderate pollution), Zn (moderate pollution), and Pb (severe contamination). The ecological risk levels of 5 heavy metals in the excess sludge from paper mill were Cr (no risk), Ni (medium risk), Cu (medium risk), Zn (low risk), and Pb (no risk). Comprehensively, among Cr, Ni, Cu, Zn, and Pb in ES, Cu and Zn have moderate pollution risks and medium ecological risks, and Pb has severe contamination risks. Moreover, the cell cytotoxicity of ES was investigated to evaluate its toxic effects on organisms if discharged into the environment without treatment. As shown in Fig. 5, when the ES concentration was only increased to 10 μg/mL, the cell viability rapidly decreased from 100 % to 77.27 %, indicating that it had certain biological toxicity. Therefore, if the ES is directly discharged into the environment, there was an environmental risk that cannot be ignored.

To investigate the effect of the resource recovery process of preparing CQDs on the environmental risks control of ES, the evaluation can be divided into two perspectives, including the removal effect of heavy metals in the synthesis process and biological toxicity assessment of synthesized ESCQDs.

The biological toxicity of ESCQDs is a factor that should be considered for nanomaterials. As shown in Fig. 5, until the concentration of ESCQDs was increased to 50 μ g/mL, the cell viability was maintained at a high level of almost 100 %. The cell viability for the ESCQD dosage at 200 μ g/mL was 88.37 %, which was higher than rice residues-derived CDs (79.11 %) [27]. When the ESCQD dosage was elevated to 500 μ g/mL, cell viability remained greater than 80 %. Moreover, compared with CQDs synthesized by chemical precursors, the biocompatibility of ESCQDs represented an advantage. As reported previously, Si-doped CQDs synthesized by hydroquinone and SiCl4 showed a cell viability of lower than 85 % when its concentration was as low as 50 μ g/mL [56], while the cell viability of ESCQDs at 50 μ g/mL was 98.12 %. Therefore, compared with CQDs synthesized by chemical precursors or other biomass, ESCQDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity, rendering applications in biological imaging and other fields. Moreover, It can be obviously seen in Fig. 5 that the biotoxicity of ESCQDs was significantly lower than that of ES, which demonstrated that synthesis of ESCQDs can effectively reduce the biological toxicity of ES.

As shown in Table 3, the removal efficiencies of 5 heavy metals during the synthesis of ESCQDs were 95.10 % of Cr, 97.33 % of Ni, 98.41 % of Cu, 98.95 % of Zn, and 99.12 % of Pb, respectively. The ecological risk levels of 5 heavy metals in the ESCQDs were all very slight contamination. Therefore, synthesis of ESCQDs efficiently removed residual heavy metals from ES and significantly reduced and control the environmental risk of heavy metals Table 4.

To figure out the structural characterization of CDs, XPS and FTIR were used to confirm the presence of chemical composition and functional groups on CDs. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the FTIR analysis showed distinct peaks at 3416, 1590, 1092, 1402, and 603 cm−1, corresponding to the N-H stretching vibrations, CO, —COO, C-N, and —NH2 functional groups, respectively [57]. Compared with the surface structure of the excess sludge (ES) before the hydrothermal reaction, ESCQDs exhibited different functional groups on their surface, particularly with more nitrogen-containing functional groups. Moreover, the four major peaks at 531.74 eV, 284.98 eV, 399.81 eV, and 347.09 eV showed up in XPS survey spectra (Fig. 6(b)), corresponding to the O 1 s, C 1 s, N 1 s and Ca 2p, respectively. Therefore, the major elements of ESCQDs were O, C, N, and Ca. It can be concluded that nitrogen-calcium (N-Ca) was successfully co-doped into the ESCQDs during the synthesis process [55]., which can also be intuitively seen from the elemental distribution mapping of TEM-EDS (Fig. S4).

As depicted in high-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s bands (Fig. 6(c)), the obtained peaks of excess sludge (ES) were different from those of ESCQDs. In the C 1 s bands of ESCQDs, three distinct peaks at 284.52, 286.05, and 287.89 were attributed to CC, C-N, and carbonyl, respectively [58,59]. In the C 1 s bands of ES, three distinct peaks at 286.31, 289.55, and 287.84 corresponded to the C-OH, O-CO, and carbonyl, respectively [22,58]. Compared with the ES, ESCQDs formed CC bonds, which were consistent with their graphite structure (Fig. 3(d)). In the O 1 s bands of ESCQDs, the two distinct peaks represented C-O (531.12 eV) and CO (532.21 eV), respectively [22]. While the two distinct peaks corresponding to the C-O (531.28 eV) and C-O-C (533.08 eV) were shown in the O1s bands of ES. Combining the C 1 s bands and O 1 s bands of ES and ESCQDs, the disappearance of C-OH, C-O-C, O-CO, and formation of carbonyl may be attributed to dehydration condensation during the hydrothermal process. To further investigate the formation of N-Ca doping, the high-resolution XPS spectra of the N 1 s bands and Ca 2p bands were shown in Fig. 6.e and f. As shown in Fig. 6(f), the Ca 2p bands had two peaks at 347.15 and 350.71 eV, corresponding to Ca 2p3/2 and Ca2p1/2, respectively [22,60]. As shown in Fig. 6(e), three distinct peaks at 399.60, 399.03, and 401.06 eV in the N1s bands of ESCQDs were attributed to pyridinic nitrogen (C-N-H), pyridinic nitrogen, and graphitic nitrogen (N-H), respectively [57,61]. The graphitic nitrogen corresponded to N-doping within the core of ESCQDs, while the pyridinic nitrogen existed either in the core or on the surface to cause defects and increase the fluorescence yields [58]. Moreover, the unshared electron pair on the pyridinic nitrogen can form hydrogen bonds with water, explaining the excellent water solubility exhibited by ESCQDs.

Formation mechanisms of CQDs remained elusive due to complex process involved, considering the variance of precursors, synthetic methods, reaction time, et al. Advancing the understanding of the formation mechanism is pivotal for CQDs investigations. In this study, we investigated the possible formation process of ESCQDs by tracking chemical features of excess sludge and ESCQDs (Fig. 6) and the formation and changes of fluorophores during hydrothermal reactions ( Fig. 7).

As the hydrothermal reaction proceeds shown in Fig. 7, the ESCQDs gradually formed and exhibited fluorescence, and formed two fluorophores at 8 h, indicating that the ESCQDs were formed at 8 h. However, the fluorophore on the right side fluctuated at the 16 h and stabilized again at the 20 h. Combined with the UV–vis results (Fig. S3 (a)), it was found that the CC bond signal was significantly enhanced during this time, indicating a significant increase in carbonation. After 20 h, the CC bond signal stabilized and the fluorophores of ESCQDs stabilized. Moreover, starting from 20 h, the fluorescence of ESCQDs gradually increased with the increase of hydrothermal time, which was consistent with the QY mentioned above (Fig. 4(c)). Therefore, it is speculated that the luminescence center was located in the graphene inner core of ESCQDs.

As Fig. 8 revealed, the ESCQDs consists of the graphite inner core and the amorphous oxygen-containing outer shell. During this process, the ES firstly underwent an aromatization reaction and formed aromatic clusters. When the concentration of these aromatic clusters reached the critical point, burst nucleation occur and trigger self-assembling into sheet graphene frameworks by carbonization and dehydration, as evident by the formation of CC bonds (Fig. 6(c)). At the same time, intermolecular and intramolecular dehydration and polymerization of carboxyl groups and ester groups in the ES can form amide groups, which was supported by the occurrence of amide bonds (-NH2) in ESCQDs (Fig. 6(a)). As the hydrothermal reaction continued, some amides can be further dehydrated with the adjacent hydroxyl group, generating pyridinic nitrogen (Figs. 8(a) and 6(e)), while some other amides can enter the graphene frameworks, generating graphitic nitrogen (Figs. 8(c) and 6(e)). As revealed by previous studies, the occurrence of graphitic N can improve the fluorescence quantum yields of CQDs [58], supporting the high QY of the ESCQDs in this study. At the same time, calcium atoms were doped in the ESCQDs in form of CaCO3 during the synthesis process (Fig. 6(f)). While the inner core was formed, residues, such as hydrophilic and oxygen-containing fragments, remained in the solution. These residues can further form surface functional groups in the outer shell. As shown in Fig. 6(c and d), carbonyl group was formed by the dehydration of hydroxyl, ester and carboxylic acid. Zeng et al. found that the introduction of carbonyl groups into carbon nitride can specifically produce singlet oxygen, singlet oxygen can be efficiently sterilized under visible light [62]. ESCQDs itself contained carbonyl groups, no external introduction was needed, thus it may have potential application advantages in the field of photodynamic sterilization. In addition, the halogen atoms (Cl/Br/I) were removed after the hydrothermal reaction (Fig. 6(a)). dehalogenation process is able to alleviate the spin-orbit coupling effect and then enhanced the fluorescence intensity of CDs [63], which could be another reason for the higher QY of ESCQDs as compared to CQDs synthesized from other biomass/waste.

The QY of Ca-N co-doped ESCQDs formed in this paper was obviously higher than that of other N-doped CD obtained in previous studies [27,28]. Does the introduction of Ca-doped structure have an effect on the improvement of the fluorescence properties of N-doped CDs? To verify this effect, ESCQDs without Ca-doped structures were prepared with ES from an urban sewage treatment plant. As shown in Fig.S6 (b), the QY of the ESCQDs without Ca-doped structured was only 8.73 %, which was far lower than the QY of ESCQDs with Ca-N co-doped structures. Therefore, it could be concluded that the formation of Ca-doping can enhance the fluorescence quantum yield of ESCQDs, and the high calcium content of ES from paper mill itself makes it an advantageous precursor for the formation of high fluorescent Ca-doped CQDs.

High fluorescence yield N-Ca co-doped ESCQDs were successfully synthesized via the facile and one-pot hydrothermal reaction without catalysts or other additives for the first time. This study clarified the times of ESCQDs formation and stabilization, elucidated parts of the synthesis mechanism, and assessed the impact of the synthesis process as well as the synthetic CQDs on the controls of heavy metals in ES. The specific contribution of this study could be concluded as follows:
(1)
The fluorescence quantum yield and mass recovery rate of ESCQDs were as high as 28.47 % and 2.86 %, respectively, which had great potential for scaling up the production of high-fluorescence-yield CQDs.
(2)
For optical properties, the synthetic ESCQDs showed excellent fluorescence properties in regards of intensity and stability. For morphological properties, the
ESCQDs possessed excellent monodispersity. Moreover, the ESCQDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity and good water solubility, rendering applications in biological imaging and other fields.
(3)
The enhancement of fluorescence performance by calcium doping was. confirmed, thereby revealed that the excess sludge was an advantageous precursor for preparing high fluorescence quantum yield Ca-N co-doped CQDs, and provided a structural suggestion for the preparation of high fluorescent CQDs
(4)
As the hydrothermal reaction proceeded, the ESCQDs were formed at 8 h, and the luminescent centers of ESCQDs were stabilized at 20 h. Among them, besides calcium doping formation, two other pathways were found to contribute to the high fluorescence yield of ESCQDs, including the formation of graphitic nitrogen doped into the core and the dehalogenation process.
(5)
Synthesis of ESCQDs efficiently removed residual heavy metals from ES and significantly reduced and control the environmental risk of heavy metals.
